title: M01 Quiz — System Design Before Code

questions.question: Which of the following is one of Meridian's three load-bearing constraints?
questions.explanation: Yjs CRDT requires browser-only state. This constraint eliminates SSR frameworks like Next.js and forces a Vite SPA architecture.

questions.question: Why is Next.js incompatible with Meridian?
options: Next.js is slower than Vite for development
Server-side rendering conflicts with Yjs CRDT, which requires browser-only state
questions.answer: Server-side rendering conflicts with Yjs CRDT, which requires browser-only state
questions.explanation: Collaborative editing state in Yjs lives in the browser. SSR would attempt to render this state on the server where it cannot exist.

questions.question: What does an Architecture Decision Record (ADR) document?
options: A list of bugs found during code review
A technology choice with context, decision, rationale, and consequences
questions.answer: A technology choice with context, decision, rationale, and consequences
questions.explanation: An ADR captures why a choice was made (not just what was chosen) and what it commits the project to. It makes implicit decisions explicit and auditable.

questions.question: Name the three load-bearing constraints that determine Meridian's entire technology stack.
questions.answer_key: 1. Browser-only collaborative editor (Lexical + Yjs CRDT). 2. Below-app-layer tenant isolation (PostgreSQL RLS). 3. In-database vector storage (pgvector).
questions.rubric: 1 point each for correctly identifying and briefly explaining each constraint. Full credit requires all three.

questions.question: What is the 'Consequences' section of an ADR for, and why is it important?
questions.answer_key: The Consequences section describes what the technology choice commits the project to: what becomes easier, what becomes harder, and what is no longer possible. It is important because it forces the author to think beyond the immediate decision to its long-term effects.
questions.rubric: Full credit: explains that Consequences documents long-term implications and commitments. Partial credit: mentions it describes trade-offs without the 'commitment' aspect.
